messages.content:
Bad fad - Ruby Tandoh on how clean eating turned toxic
The backlash against a 'wellness' diet is in full swing - and leading chefs and writers associated with it are doing their best to ditch the term. It's time we called it what it is.

[1] 'I think we have an obsession with diets. With fad diets?' This was Ella Mills, the food writer behind Deliciously Ella and bestselling author of arguably the most successful fad diet cookbook series in recent years. Her eponymous first book was the UK's fastest-selling debut cookbook ever, and she has since gone on to launch two central-London delis, create her own range of 'energy balls' and even release a skincare range in collaboration with Neal's Yard. Speaking on last Friday's Today programme, the blogger was keen to distance herself from so-called 'clean eating' and, cleverly, from the crescendo of criticism surrounding the trend.
[2] 'T've never described myself as "clean", said Mills, nimbly shadow-boxing with a litheness that probably shouldn't surprise us, coming from a 25-year-old yogi. She described her frustration at being dubbed the 'queen of clean', and was quick to point out the moralistic overtones that language such as 'clean eating' ascribes to food. It was a smart move, preempting the backlash that would come from that evening's premiere of the BBC documentary Horizon: Clean Eating - The Dirty Truth.

[3] This new philosophy, popularised by bloggers such as Mills, the Hemsley sisters and Amelia Freer, was roughly the idea that we should eat less processed food and more fruit and veg. It was about doing away with junk food, and staging a return to a more thoughtful way of eating. So far, so sensible, you might think. Although not all of the bloggers used the exact phrase 'clean eating', their approaches fell into step with its core beliefs: slimness is health, health is everything, and - crucially - diets are bad.
[4] Cannily dodging the restrictive and judgement-laden overtones of the dreaded D-word, this generation of cooks was vocal in eschewing old-fashioned diet terminology. The Hemsley sisters' grain-free manifesto, The Art of Eating Well, wasn't a diet book, then, but a way to achieve some kind of seductively slinky 'wellness'. Healthy-eating guru
Madeleine Shaw encouraged us not to count calories, but to 'get the glow'. 'This isn't a diet for me; it's a lifestyle,' Mills told us. It was a world of beauty, happiness and self-love, all bundled lucratively together under the vague banner of wellness.
[5] With their profits and media presence blossoming, however, it wasn't long before these self-appointed health gurus came under scrutiny.
[6] Natasha Corrett, author of the bestseller Honestly Healthy, was embarrassed last week by the revelation that the inspiration for her alkaline diet, Dr Robert O Young, is facing jail time for practising medicine without a licence.
Horizon revealed that Young took more than £62 000 in payment from the family of cancer patient Naima Houder-Mohammed in return for a 'cure' of injections of bicarbonate of soda solution. Houder-Mohammed's condition worsened during her time in Young's care and she died at 27. Alongside such outright crooks and villains as Young, the hapless
 Mills doesn't look so bad[7] Behind the pretty public face of wellness is a far bigger beast. With the exception of fitness guru Joe Wicks, the overwhelming majority of wellness personalities are young women, and it is these women who rise to and eventually fall from grace in the public eye. And yet the machinery of these fads is constructed largely by a small group of men.
These are the doctors - self-styled or otherwise - who spin questionable academic studies, patchworks of data and sometimes little more than fanciful anecdotes into best-selling diet industry manifestos.
[8] A share of the blame lies with publishers, too. At a time when money is rare in publishing, editors are digging deep to offer book deals to wellness gurus, no matter the credibility of their claims.
[9] There are many toxic layers to the wellness phenomenon. It is no coincidence that the faces of wellness are unfailingly young and thin, overwhelmingly white and all the talk of purity against that backdrop of privilege leaves a rather unsavoury taste in the mouth. Wellness is also the unprincipled driftings of the publishing houses with whatever currents lead them to a quick buck. It is the evangelism of wellness bloggers, picking up and shrugging off whatever label will give them the most page views. At its core is the idea that food is medicine.
[10] But these sensational claims reach us by insidious means, and those who suggest we ought to just use some common sense underestimate just how muddled the truth about food really is. So, many of us will, at one point or another, turn to the false prophets of the diet industry. We might become evangelists for wheatgrass for a while, or sing the praises of some superfood that 'cured' a dodgy knee. We might laugh these fads off, in time, just as the wellness personalities slip out of their skins and into the next big craze.
[11] We have an obsession with diets. With fad diets. There is no miracle cure, but there is a way to push back: call them as you see them. Wellness is a fad diet. Clean eating is a fad diet. If it promises salvation on a plate, it is a fad diet.
Hold the wellness gurus accountable, let them know that their diet is a diet. Make them eat their words.

This article critically examines the phenomenon known as "clean eating" and the broader wellness trend, highlighting its pernicious aspects. The discourse around wellness and clean eating has evolved over time, beginning as an encouragement for healthier eating habits—focusing on less processed foods and more fruits and vegetables. However, as the movement gained popularity through social media and various influencers, it morphed into something more insidious, linked with unattainable beauty standards, questionable health advice, and a commercial industry ready to profit from these trends.

Ella Mills (Deliciously Ella) and other high-profile figures associated with the clean eating movement have attempted to distance themselves from the term due to the increasing backlash and revelation of harmful practices within the industry. This distancing also comes in light of the damaging influence of certain individuals promoting pseudoscientific health advice for profit, such as Dr. Robert O Young, whose practices have had dire consequences for individuals seeking health solutions.

The article brings to light the problematic elements of wellness culture, including its exclusivity (predominantly young, thin, white individuals), its foundation on dubious scientific claims, and the commercial interests driving its popularity rather than genuine health benefits. The wellness phenomenon is critiqued for its cyclical nature of capitalizing on new health trends without substantiated benefits, leading to a toxic environment for both consumers and those promoting these lifestyles, knowingly or unwittingly.

Furthermore, the piece suggests that to combat the misleading narratives of the wellness industry, there is a need for a critical examination of these wellness claims and holding those who promote them accountable. In essence, calling out wellness and clean eating as fad diets is a step towards acknowledging their lack of scientific foundation and the potential harm they can cause, advocating for a more sensible and inclusive approach to health and nutrition.

This article, therefore, serves as a call to action to critically appraise the wellness industry, recognize the potentially harmful aspects of fad diets, and encourage a more balanced and inclusive understanding of health that moves away from restrictive and morally loaded concepts of eating.

42. What is the 'bad fad' being referred to in the title?
43. Find a phrase in paragraph 1 that has a similar meaning to 'The backlash against a "wellness" diet is in full swing' (sub-headline).

42. The 'bad fad' being referred to in the title is the concept of "clean eating" that turned toxic. This refers to the trend and popular movement around eating a diet considered 'pure', 'natural', and free from processed foods, which, while initially starting with positive intentions, evolved into a problematic phenomenon with harmful practices, unrealistic beauty and health standards, and commercial exploitation.

43. A phrase in paragraph 1 that has a similar meaning to 'The backlash against a "wellness" diet is in full swing' is "and, cleverly, from the crescendo of criticism surrounding the trend." This part of the sentence refers to how individuals like Ella Mills, associated with the clean eating movement, are trying to distance themselves from the term 'clean eating' due to the mounting criticism and backlash the trend has received for its toxic practices and implications.

56. In paragraphs 7 and 8, what THREE groups have driven the popularity of wellness diets?
(3 marks)
i)
ii)
iii)

The three groups that have driven the popularity of wellness diets, as mentioned in paragraphs 7 and 8, are:

i) Self-styled or otherwise doctors who manipulate questionable academic studies and sometimes fanciful anecdotes into best-selling diet industry manifestos.

ii) Publishers who are willing to offer book deals to wellness gurus regardless of the credibility of their claims, seeking profit in a challenging publishing market.

iii) Wellness personalities, particularly the bloggers and influencers who promote these wellness trends for page views and social media presence, despite the changing nature of their endorsements.
shortly

The three groups driving the popularity of wellness diets are:

i) Questionable doctors
ii) Publishers
iii) Wellness influencers/bloggers
57. What is the writer's opinion of the 'small group of men' (line 33). Explain your answer using an example given in the text.

The writer's opinion of the "small group of men" is clearly negative, viewing them as a major part of the problem behind the propagation and credibility of wellness diets. This group is described as constructing the machinery of wellness fads largely for profit, often at the expense of public health and truth.

An example given in the text to illustrate this negative view is the mention of self-styled doctors who "spin questionable academic studies, patchworks of data and sometimes little more than fanciful anecdotes into best-selling diet industry manifestos." This points to a manipulation of information and a disregard for scientific integrity, indicating the writer's criticism of these individuals for prioritizing profit over genuine health advice and contributing to the misleading and harmful aspects of wellness culture.
shortly

The writer views the "small group of men" negatively, seeing them as contributors to the misleading aspects of wellness culture. This is exemplified by their manipulation of data into best-selling diet books, prioritizing profit over legitimate health advice.
58. Describe the common features of those with 'the faces of wellness' (line 38) based on paragraph 9.

Based on paragraph 9, the common features of those with "the faces of wellness" include being young and thin, overwhelmingly white, and often involved in discussions about purity and health. This description highlights a lack of diversity and suggests an exclusive image associated with the wellness culture, which may unintentionally promote unrealistic beauty and health standards.
shortly shortly
The "faces of wellness" are typically young, thin, and overwhelmingly white, presenting an exclusive and unrealistic image of health.
59. Name ONE food example mentioned in paragraph 10 that plays on 'the idea that food is medicine' (line 42).
One food example mentioned in paragraph 10 that plays on "the idea that food is medicine" is wheatgrass.

60. Which of the following describes how the writer feels in paragraph 11?
A. determined to correct something that is wrong
B. satisfied with the backlash against fad diets
C. hesitant to state her opinion too explicitly
D. obsessed with misgivings about dieting
A. determined to correct something that is wrong

62. Match the subheadings to the outline for Text 3. Write A-F in the boxes next to the numbers. One subheading is NOT used.
(5 marks)
Subheadings
A. The wellness backers
B. Actual healthy eating
C. Not-a-diet diets
D. Severing ties with 'clean'
E. Facing our confusion
F. No longer scot-free

Given the information and themes presented in the detailed description of the text, the best matches for the subheadings to the outline for Text 3 could be inferred as follows:

1. D. Severing ties with 'clean' - This could relate to where Ella Mills and others are trying to distance themselves from the term 'clean eating' due to the backlash.

2. B. Actual healthy eating - This might fit best with the description of the initial idea of the wellness philosophy, advocating for less processed food and more fruits and vegetables.

3. C. Not-a-diet diets - This likely matches with the narrative around wellness influencers positioning their dietary advice not as diets but as lifestyle changes, promoting an image of wellness outside traditional diet culture.

4. A. The wellness backers - This could be associated with the discussion about the groups driving the popularity of wellness diets, including questionable doctors, publishers, and wellness personalities.

5. E. Facing our confusion - Given the context, this could align with the conclusion and the call to action for a more critical engagement with the wellness industry and recognizing fad diets. 

Please note that without direct access to the outline of Text 3 and based solely on the information provided in the detailed descriptions before, these matches are speculative. F. No longer scot-free is the subheading NOT used based on the selection above.

Wellness superfoods that aren't actually that super
- according to dietitian Helen Bond
[12] Coconut oil: 'One of the few plant oils that is loaded with saturated fat - it has more saturated fat than butter.
While advocates argue that it doesn't have the same detrimental impact on blood cholesterol levels, it still raises total and LDL (bad cholesterol) in our blood.'
55
[13] Matcha powder: 'No health claims have been approved for matcha or green tea by the European Food Safety Authority, so it cannot be advertised as having any proven health benefits.'
[14] Coconut water: 'A 200ml glass would provide the same amount of potassium as one medium banana. Tap water will hydrate you just the same and it is calorie and sugar free.'
Source: adapted from Ruby Tandoh's 'Bad fad - Ruby Tandoh on how clean eating turned toxic', theguardian.com, 23 January 2017.

Based on dietitian Helen Bond's opinions, here are wellness superfoods that aren't as beneficial as often claimed:

1. **Coconut Oil:** Contrary to popular belief in its health benefits, coconut oil is high in saturated fat, even more than butter. While some argue it doesn't impact blood cholesterol levels as other saturated fats might, it still increases total and LDL (bad) cholesterol levels.

2. **Matcha Powder:** Despite the trends and numerous health claims associated with matcha or green tea, no health claims have been officially approved by the European Food Safety Authority. Therefore, it cannot be legitimately marketed for having specific health benefits.

3. **Coconut Water:** Often advertised as a superior hydrating beverage, a 200ml glass of coconut water contains a similar amount of potassium to one medium banana. However, plain tap water can hydrate equally well without the additional calories and sugar that coconut water contains.

63. According to the text, health claims made about matcha powder ...
A. will be disproved.
B. have been approved.
C. are being investigated.
D. may not be used in ads.
D. may not be used in ads.

64. Complete the following table by putting a tick (V) in the correct boxes.

iv) Coconut water has calories -
Coconut water is hydrating -
i) Butter has saturated fat - ii) A banana has potassium -






less than




as much as




more than
tap water.
tap water.
200 ml coconut water.
coconut oil.

Here's the completed table based on the information provided:

|                                     | less than       | as much as      | more than       |
|-------------------------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| iv) Coconut water has calories      |                 |                 | V (tap water)   |
| Coconut water is hydrating          |                 | V (tap water)   |                 |
| i) Butter has saturated fat         |                 |                 | V (coconut oil) |
| ii) A banana has potassium          |                 | V (200 ml coconut water) |                 |
65. Why do you think the sub-headline mentions 'dietitian Helen Band'?

The sub-headline mentions "dietitian Helen Bond" to lend credibility and authority to the critique of certain wellness superfoods. By specifying that the insights come from a dietitian, it signals to readers that the information is based on professional knowledge and expertise in nutrition. This helps to differentiate the provided information from the myriad of unverified health claims circulating in the wellness community, ensuring that the critique of these superfoods is grounded in scientifically informed perspectives rather than personal opinions or popular fads.
shorty shortly
Mentioning "dietitian Helen Bond" lends credibility, highlighting that the critique is based on professional expertise in nutrition.
